# Architecture Policy

## Purpose

Define the default architecture selection rule for AI-assisted workflow agents.

Core decision:

- simple first;
- avoid overengineering by default;
- escalate architecture only when justified.

## Mandatory decision rule

Before creating files, classify the work as one of:

- `simple-site`
- `feature-app`
- `domain-app`
- `package-tooling`
- `wordpress`

If unsure, choose the lower-complexity level and document why.

## Architecture levels

## Level 1 — Simple App Structure (`simple-site`)

Use for landing pages, portfolios, static/documentation sites, marketing pages, and prototypes.

Recommended structure:

- `components/ui`
- `components/layout`
- `components/sections`
- `data`
- `pages`
- `assets`

## Level 2 — Feature-first Modular (`feature-app`)

Use for apps with multiple features, API calls, authentication, dashboards, forms, and CRUD.

Recommended structure:

- `features/<feature>/components`
- `features/<feature>/composables`
- `features/<feature>/services`
- `features/<feature>/stores`
- `features/<feature>/types.ts`

Rules:

- keep feature logic close to each feature;
- shared/global folders only when reused by at least two features;
- do not introduce domain/application/infrastructure layers by default.

## Level 3 — Clean/Hexagonal per Module (`domain-app`)

Use only when a module has business rules, multiple data sources/integrations, non-trivial workflows, or needs isolated domain testing.

Recommended structure:

- `domain`
- `application`
- `infrastructure`
- `presentation`

Dependency rules:

- `domain` must not import framework/UI/HTTP/store/router code;
- `application` may depend on `domain`;
- `infrastructure` implements ports/interfaces;
- `presentation` depends on `application`.

Flow target:

`UI → composable/store → service/use-case → repository interface → adapter/API`

## Level 4 — Package/Starter Architecture (`package-tooling`)

Use for libraries, CLIs, reusable packages, workflow kits, presets, templates, validators, and npm-package-style projects.

Recommended structure:

- `core`
- `cli`
- `presets`
- `templates`
- `validators`
- `docs`
- `examples`

## Level 5 — WordPress Modular (`wordpress`)

Use for WordPress themes/plugins.

Recommended structure:

- `inc`
- `template-parts`
- `blocks`
- `acf`
- `assets`
- `languages`
- `tests` (if applicable)

Rules:

- use hooks/actions/filters explicitly;
- separate template rendering from query/business logic where practical;
- preserve existing PHP compatibility;
- do not force Clean Architecture unless domain complexity justifies it.

## Escalation criteria (Level 2 → Level 3)

Escalate from Feature-first to Clean/Hexagonal only if at least **2** are true:

- business rules are independent from UI;
- data source may change;
- mock and real adapters are needed;
- multiple APIs/integrations exist;
- isolated unit tests are required;
- domain language is stable and important;
- module is expected to grow significantly.

## Decision matrix

| Context | Default architecture |
| --- | --- |
| Landing page / portfolio / static site | Level 1 — Simple App Structure |
| Small app with API | Level 2 — Feature-first Modular |
| Dashboard/admin app | Level 2 — Feature-first Modular |
| SaaS with business rules | Level 3 — Clean/Hexagonal per module |
| Multiple APIs/adapters/integrations | Level 3 — Clean/Hexagonal per module |
| Library/CLI/workflow starter/package | Level 4 — Package/Starter Architecture |
| WordPress theme/plugin | Level 5 — WordPress Modular |

## Forbidden defaults

Do not introduce these unless required by the selected architecture level:

- repositories
- use-cases
- entities
- DTOs
- mappers
- dependency injection containers
- abstract factories
- domain services
- specification pattern
- global service locators
